title: Altermagnons at the metal-insulator transition
authors: Jonas Issing, Matteo Dürrnagel, Sarbajit Mazumdar, Alena Lorenz, Niklas Witt, Giorgio Sangiovanni, Michael Klett, Lennart Klebl, Ronny Thomale, Jannis Seufert
abstract: By means of slave-boson theory for the Hubbard model on the checkerboard lattice, we calculate dynamical altermagnetic spin susceptibilities from the metallic to the Mott-insulating regime. We track magnon dispersion and lifetime renormalization, allowing us to uncover a crossover from a chirality-selective dissipation of magnon modes to coherent yet strongly deformed chiral magnon branches across the metal insulator transition. Our formalism lends itself to a quantitative description of collective spin dynamics in correlated altermagnets.
summary: This paper provides a unified theoretical description of spin dynamics in altermagnets from the Mott insulating to the metallic regime. It reveals that the metal-insulator transition triggers a crossover between a regime of selective magnon damping and a regime of coherent, albeit deformed, chiral magnons.
main_problem: Investigating the stability and dynamical spin response of altermagnetic quasiparticles (chiral magnons) across the metal-insulator transition.
main_result: The study identifies a crossover from chirality-selective dissipation of magnon modes in the metallic regime to coherent but strongly deformed chiral magnon branches in the insulating regime.
method: Spin-rotationally invariant Kotliar-Ruckenstein slave-boson theory (SBMFT) used to calculate dynamical spin susceptibilities and evaluate Gaussian fluctuations.
model_or_system: A one-band Hubbard model on a checkerboard lattice with anisotropic next-nearest-neighbor hopping (t_plus and t_minus) to induce altermagnetism.
key_observables: Dynamical transverse spin susceptibility (Im[chi_S_perp]), magnon dispersion, magnon lifetime, and magnon chirality (C(q, omega)).
important_parameters: On-site Coulomb interaction (U), hopping anisotropy (delta), and the transition between paramagnetic metal, altermagnetic metal, and altermagnetic insulator phases.
main_assumptions: The calculation is performed at the Gaussian level of fluctuations around a static two-sublattice saddle-point ansatz.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the checkerboard lattice schematic; Figure 2 displays the imaginary part of the transverse spin susceptibility and chirality-resolved spectra; Supplementary figures compare RPA, LSWT, and SBMFT results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Hubbard model on a checkerboard lattice, defines the slave-boson formalism and effective action, presents the phase diagram and metal-insulator transition, analyzes the dynamical spin response and magnon damping, and concludes with a comparison of theoretical methods.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Checkerboard square lattice Hubbard model with anisotropic diagonal hoppings t± = t′ ± δ. (b) Slave-boson mean-field phase diagram in the U–δ plane, comprising a paramagnetic metal (PM–M), an antiferromagnetic insulator (AFM–I), an altermagnetic metal (AM–M), and an altermag- netic insulator (AM–I). The hatched area marks an unsta- ble region before the metal–insulator transition. (c) Spin- polarized quasiparticle band structures for the PM–M, AM– M, and AM–I phases at δ = 0.2, plotted along the high- symmetry path shown in the inset.

FIG. 2. Top row: Imaginary part of the transverse spin susceptibility, Im [χ⊥ S ], along the high-symmetry path Γ–X–M–Γ for different values of U at δ = 0.2 corresponding to the AM–I (a) far away from the MIT and (b) close to the MIT, as well as (c) the AM–M phase. The magnon dispersion obtained from linear spin-wave theory (LSWT) is shown by the black dotted lines in panel (a). Bottom row: Chirality-resolved magnon spectrum for the respective phases. Both magnons are degenerate along the nodal line M–Γ, such that their chiralities cancel each other, while each individual magnon branch as well as the associated continua remain chiral.

FIG. 3. Imaginary (first row) and real parts (second row) of the transverse spin susceptibility, χ⊥ s (Q, ω), together with the chirality-resolved joint density of states (JDOS; bottom two rows) for spin-flip excitations, cf. Eq. (S1.17), across the AFM–AM transition for increasing hopping anisotropy δ, shown close to the metal–insulator transition at (a) U = 3.5, δ = 0, (b) U = 3.9, δ = 0.1, and (c) U = 4.6, δ = 0.2. Upon increasing δ, the interaction between magnons and the Stoner continuum evolves in an anisotropic fashion, resulting in momentum-dependent magnon stability.
